patch.target: Waikiki: easy for first-timers, steps to food and main beaches. Ala Moana: better value, easier for relaxed group hangouts. Kailua: only practical if you get a rental; ideal for groups set on kayaking/beach mornings.

patch.after: Skip Waikiki unless someone in the group has never seen Hawaii—Ala Moana rentals are cheaper, quieter, and you still walk to everything.

TL;DR is 70 words (target 40–58)

1593 words is below pillar minimum 1900 (target 2500)

Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated

Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
AI-signature phrases present (1): the key to success is
Specific human voice vs committee 4/10 — Reads like marketing copy with TL;DR, Key takeaways, FAQ scaffolding; occasional first-person flashes but mostly institutional.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — Some real takes (rentals beat hotels, Rainbow Drive-In) but mostly hedged, evenhanded advice.
Unexpected phrasings 5/10 — "Herding cats," "hangry mutinies," "sane, sunburned" show some life, but most sentences are travel-blog boilerplate.
Point of view vs both-sides 6/10 — Has a mild POV but the FAQ and comparison sections hedge into neutral surveying.
Quotable sentences 5/10 — The group-chat opening and plate-lunch closer are quotable; most of the body isn't.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with meta-chrome (TL;DR, byline, date, read-time) before any hook; actual hook is buried paragraphs down.

specificity 6/10 — The cost section uses ranges and categories correctly, but the framing sentence is pure throat-clearing abstraction ('nobody likes to talk money,' 'no fluff'). More critically, the post never anchors costs to specific vendors or transaction examples — it lists line items without showing the math in context. The rewrite grounds the same numbers in named places and real per-unit prices, making the figures feel earned rather than estimated.
